Front:
Your organization deploys a hub-spoke topology with a hub VNet (10.0.0.0/16) and 20 spoke VNets (10.1.0.0/16 through 10.20.0.0/16). Each spoke needs to communicate with shared services in the hub and with each other. Which routing configuration enables this full-mesh communication?

A) Spoke-to-spoke VNet peering without hub involvement
B) Hub VNet peering with gateway transit and Allow Forwarded Traffic enabled
C) No peering configuration needed
Back: B) Hub VNet peering with gateway transit and Allow Forwarded Traffic enabled
Extra:
Hub-spoke topology with gateway transit allows spokes to communicate through hub using UDRs or virtual network gateway transit. Allow Forwarded Traffic permits spoke-to-spoke via hub. Direct spoke-to-spoke peering creates management complexity with 190 peerings. No peering prevents communication.

Reference: Hub-spoke network topology

Front:
A VNet requires IP address space for 500 VMs, 50 Azure Firewall instances, 10 Application Gateways, and Azure Bastion. VMs need /24 subnet, firewalls need /26, Application Gateways need /27, and Bastion needs /26. Which VNet CIDR provides sufficient capacity with minimal waste?

A) /16 providing 65,536 addresses
B) /20 providing 4,096 addresses
C) /24 providing 256 addresses
Back: B) /20 providing 4,096 addresses
Extra:
/20 provides 4,096 IPs: VMs need 512 (/23 or two /24s), Firewalls 64 (/26), App GW 128 (4x /27 minimum), Bastion 64 (/26) = ~768 + Azure reserved ~5% = under 1000, fitting in /20. /16 wastes 60K+ addresses. /24 insufficient for requirements.

Reference: VNet sizing

Front:
Your organization owns public IP range 203.0.113.0/24 and wants to use it in Azure instead of Azure-provided public IPs for regulatory compliance. Which Azure feature enables bringing your own IP addresses?

A) Standard public IP addresses without custom range
B) Custom public IP address prefix (BYOIP) with range attestation
C) Private IP addresses only
Back: B) Custom public IP address prefix (BYOIP) with range attestation
Extra:
Custom IP prefix (BYOIP) allows bringing customer-owned public IP ranges to Azure after ownership verification. Standard Azure public IPs use Microsoft-owned ranges. Private IPs don't provide public internet connectivity required.

Reference: Bring your own IP

Front:
A multi-region application requires DNS resolution where users connect to the nearest healthy region. The solution must automatically remove failed regions from DNS within 30 seconds. Which Azure DNS configuration achieves this?

A) Azure DNS public zone with static A records
B) Azure Traffic Manager with performance routing and health probes
C) Azure Private DNS without monitoring
Back: B) Azure Traffic Manager with performance routing and health probes
Extra:
Traffic Manager provides DNS-based global traffic distribution with performance routing directing users to nearest region and health probes automatically removing unhealthy endpoints. Static DNS lacks health monitoring. Private DNS doesn't support public internet routing.

Reference: Traffic Manager routing

Front:
Your solution requires private DNS resolution between Azure VNets and on-premises networks with conditional forwarding for specific domain suffixes. Which Azure DNS solution provides this hybrid DNS capability?

A) Azure public DNS zones
B) Azure DNS Private Resolver with forwarding rules
C) On-premises DNS only
Back: B) Azure DNS Private Resolver with forwarding rules
Extra:
Azure DNS Private Resolver enables centralized DNS resolution with conditional forwarding between Azure and on-premises, supporting private DNS zones and custom forwarding rules. Public zones don't resolve private names. On-premises-only DNS can't resolve Azure private DNS zones.

Reference: Azure DNS Private Resolver

Front:
A VNet requires a subnet delegated to Azure SQL Managed Instance. The managed instance needs exclusive control over the subnet. Which subnet configuration parameter enables this service delegation?

A) No delegation, shared subnet with other resources
B) Subnet delegation to Microsoft.Sql/managedInstances service
C) Network security group rules only
Back: B) Subnet delegation to Microsoft.Sql/managedInstances service
Extra:
Subnet delegation grants exclusive control to specific Azure services requiring network policy management. SQL Managed Instance requires delegated subnet. Shared subnets don't provide service-required network control. NSG rules alone don't delegate subnet control.

Reference: Subnet delegation

Front:
Your organization deploys Azure Kubernetes Service and Azure App Service requiring dedicated subnets in the same VNet. Both services need outbound internet access. Which subnet design provides proper isolation and connectivity?

A) Single shared subnet for all services
B) Dedicated subnets per service with shared NAT Gateway or Azure Firewall
C) No internet connectivity configuration
Back: B) Dedicated subnets per service with shared NAT Gateway or Azure Firewall
Extra:
Dedicated subnets per service provide security isolation while shared NAT Gateway or Azure Firewall provides centralized outbound internet connectivity and IP management. Single subnet reduces isolation. Services without internet access can't download updates or access external APIs.

Reference: Azure NAT Gateway

Front:
A VNet (10.0.0.0/16) peers with another VNet (10.1.0.0/16). Traffic from 10.0.0.0/16 to specific subnet 10.1.10.0/24 must route through a firewall at 10.0.1.4. Which configuration implements this custom routing?

A) No configuration, default routing handles all traffic
B) User-defined route (UDR) with route to 10.1.10.0/24 via 10.0.1.4 appliance
C) VNet peering routing automatically applies
Back: B) User-defined route (UDR) with route to 10.1.10.0/24 via 10.0.1.4 appliance
Extra:
UDRs override default Azure routing, directing specific traffic through network virtual appliances like firewalls. Default routing uses direct VNet peering without inspection. VNet peering alone doesn't enforce traffic through security appliances.

Reference: User-defined routes

Front:
Your organization requires all VNet traffic to on-premises to route through an Azure Firewall for inspection, even VPN/ExpressRoute default routes. Which UDR configuration enforces this traffic flow?

A) No route configuration, default routes to on-premises
B) UDR with 0.0.0.0/0 to firewall and disable BGP route propagation
C) VNet peering routes only
Back: B) UDR with 0.0.0.0/0 to firewall and disable BGP route propagation
Extra:
Forced tunneling with 0.0.0.0/0 UDR to firewall and disabled BGP propagation prevents default on-premises routes from bypassing firewall. Default routes allow direct VPN/ExpressRoute without inspection. VNet peering doesn't affect on-premises routing.

Reference: Forced tunneling

Front:
An application in subnet A (10.0.1.0/24) cannot reach resources in subnet B (10.0.2.0/24) within the same VNet. Network Watcher IP flow verify shows traffic being blocked. Which diagnostic step identifies the root cause?

A) Assume routing issue without verification
B) Check NSG effective security rules on both subnet and NIC
C) Restart all VMs to fix connectivity
Back: B) Check NSG effective security rules on both subnet and NIC
Extra:
NSG effective security rules show combined result of subnet and NIC-level NSG rules identifying blocking rules. Assumptions miss actual cause. Restarting VMs doesn't address NSG misconfigurations preventing connectivity.

Reference: NSG diagnostics

Front:
Your hub-spoke topology requires spoke VNets to learn routes to on-premises via VPN gateway in the hub. Which Azure Route Server configuration enables this dynamic route propagation?

A) Static routes in each spoke VNet
B) Azure Route Server in hub peering BGP routes to spokes
C) Manual route table updates per spoke
Back: B) Azure Route Server in hub peering BGP routes to spokes
Extra:
Azure Route Server exchanges routes with network virtual appliances and propagates them to peered VNets, enabling dynamic route learning without static configuration. Static routes require manual updates per change. Manual updates don't scale and miss dynamic changes.

Reference: Azure Route Server

Front:
A VNet requires outbound internet connectivity for 1,000 VMs with consistent source IP for external firewall allow-listing. Azure Load Balancer standard already deployed. Which additional component provides outbound SNAT?

A) No additional configuration needed
B) Azure NAT Gateway providing dedicated outbound IPs and SNAT
C) Individual public IP per VM
Back: B) Azure NAT Gateway providing dedicated outbound IPs and SNAT
Extra:
NAT Gateway provides scalable outbound SNAT with dedicated public IPs for firewall allow-listing without port exhaustion. Load Balancer alone may exhaust SNAT ports. Individual IPs per VM is costly and complex to manage.

Reference: NAT Gateway for outbound

Front:
Your organization deploys Azure Virtual Network Manager to centrally manage 50 VNets across subscriptions. Security policy requires blocking traffic between dev and prod VNets while allowing dev-to-dev and prod-to-prod. Which AVNM feature implements this segmentation?

A) Manual NSG rules in each VNet
B) AVNM security admin rules with network groups
C) No centralized management possible
Back: B) AVNM security admin rules with network groups
Extra:
Azure Virtual Network Manager network groups and security admin rules provide centralized network segmentation policy across VNets. Manual NSG rules per VNet don't scale and lack centralized management. AVNM specifically designed for multi-VNet governance.

Reference: AVNM security

Front:
Network Watcher flow logs show high traffic volume from specific source IP to multiple VMs on port 3389. You need to determine if this is legitimate admin traffic or potential attack. Which analysis approach identifies this?

A) Ignore traffic analysis
B) Traffic Analytics with NSG flow logs analyzing patterns and anomalies
C) Manual log review without tools
Back: B) Traffic Analytics with NSG flow logs analyzing patterns and anomalies
Extra:
Traffic Analytics processes NSG flow logs with ML-based anomaly detection, identifying unusual patterns and potential security issues. Ignoring traffic allows undetected attacks. Manual review doesn't scale or detect subtle patterns.

Reference: Traffic Analytics

Front:
Your organization requires DDoS protection for public IP addresses with automatic attack mitigation and access to DDoS rapid response team. Which Azure DDoS protection tier provides these advanced capabilities?

A) Basic DDoS protection included free
B) DDoS Protection Standard with DRR team access
C) No DDoS protection available
Back: B) DDoS Protection Standard with DRR team access
Extra:
DDoS Protection Standard provides adaptive tuning, attack analytics, mitigation reports, and DDoS Rapid Response team engagement. Basic protection offers only infrastructure-level protection without advanced features. All Azure services have basic protection but advanced scenarios require Standard.

Reference: DDoS Protection Standard

Front:
Microsoft Defender for Cloud identifies subnet without NSG as security recommendation. The subnet contains only Azure PaaS services using service endpoints. How should this recommendation be addressed?

A) Ignore recommendation for PaaS services
B) Implement NSG with service tag rules for PaaS services
C) Remove all resources from subnet
Back: B) Implement NSG with service tag rules for PaaS services
Extra:
NSGs should protect all subnets including PaaS services using service tags for simplified management. Ignoring security recommendations increases risk. Removing resources doesn't address security requirement.

Reference: Service tags in NSGs

Front:
Network Watcher Connection Monitor shows latency spikes to on-premises database during business hours. Which diagnostics identify whether issue is Azure networking, VPN tunnel, or on-premises network?

A) Assume Azure networking issue without testing
B) Connection Monitor with multi-hop path analysis and NPM
C) No diagnostic tools available
Back: B) Connection Monitor with multi-hop path analysis and NPM
Extra:
Connection Monitor with Network Performance Monitor provides hop-by-hop latency analysis isolating issues to specific network segments (Azure, VPN, on-premises). Assumptions don't identify root cause. Azure provides comprehensive network diagnostics.

Reference: Connection Monitor

Front:
Your hub VNet has VPN gateway and Azure Firewall. Spoke VNets need to access on-premises via hub gateway and have internet traffic inspected by hub firewall. Which combination achieves both requirements?

A) VNet peering with gateway transit and UDRs to firewall for 0.0.0.0/0
B) No peering configuration
C) Direct VPN gateway in each spoke
Back: A) VNet peering with gateway transit and UDRs to firewall for 0.0.0.0/0
Extra:
Gateway transit allows spokes to use hub VPN gateway for on-premises connectivity while UDRs route internet traffic (0.0.0.0/0) through firewall for inspection. No peering prevents connectivity. Direct gateways per spoke are costly and complex.

Reference: Gateway transit

Front:
A public IP address prefix with 16 addresses (203.0.113.0/28) needs allocation to multiple Application Gateways in different regions. Which IP allocation strategy provides regional distribution?

A) Single public IP shared across regions
B) Create public IPs from prefix and assign by region
C) Private IPs only
Back: B) Create public IPs from prefix and assign by region
Extra:
Public IP prefix allows creating multiple public IPs from contiguous range, assigning them to resources by region while maintaining IP range continuity. Single IP doesn't provide regional distribution. Public IPs required for internet-facing gateways.

Reference: Public IP prefix

Front:
Your organization connects 200 branch offices to Azure using site-to-site VPN with BGP. Route advertisements from on-premises exceed VPN gateway's route limits. Which routing optimization addresses this scale limitation?

A) Disable BGP and use static routes
B) Implement route summarization aggregating routes
C) Deploy separate gateway per branch
Back: B) Implement route summarization aggregating routes
Extra:
Route summarization reduces number of advertised routes by aggregating specific routes into summary routes, staying within gateway limits. Disabling BGP loses dynamic routing benefits. Separate gateways per branch is cost-prohibitive and unmanageable.

Reference: BGP with VPN Gateway

Front:
A VNet peers with multiple other VNets in hub-spoke topology. You need to change the VNet's address space but deployment succeeds with warning about possible connectivity disruption. What is the risk?

A) No risk, changes apply immediately without impact
B) Overlapping address spaces or routing issues during transition
C) VNet address space cannot be changed
Back: B) Overlapping address spaces or routing issues during transition
Extra:
Changing VNet address space while peered can temporarily disrupt connectivity if new range overlaps with peered VNets or during route propagation. Changes aren't instant and require validation. Address spaces can be changed but require careful planning.

Reference: VNet address space changes

Front:
Azure Policy evaluation shows several VNets lack Customer Managed Keys for VNet encryption. Which remediation addresses this compliance gap?

A) Ignore VNet encryption requirement
B) Enable VNet encryption with Custom Managed Keys per VNet
C) Encryption not supported for VNets
Back: B) Enable VNet encryption with Custom Managed Keys per VNet
Extra:
VNet encryption with Customer Managed Keys provides additional data protection for virtual network traffic meeting compliance requirements. Ignoring requirements violates policy. VNet encryption is supported and recommended for sensitive workloads.

Reference: VNet encryption

Front:
Network Watcher shows packets being dropped between two VMs in different subnets. Next Hop troubleshooting shows correct routing. Which additional diagnostic identifies the blocking point?

A) Assume network issue without further diagnostics
B) IP Flow Verify checking NSG rules at subnet and NIC level
C) Restart VMs to resolve
Back: B) IP Flow Verify checking NSG rules at subnet and NIC level
Extra:
IP Flow Verify tests whether packet is allowed or denied by NSG rules, showing specific rule blocking traffic. Next Hop shows routing but not security filtering. Assumptions and restarts don't identify NSG misconfigurations.

Reference: IP Flow Verify

Front:
Your organization implements Azure Bastion requiring dedicated subnet. The Bastion subnet cannot have NSG associated. Which subnet name and minimum size are required?

A) Any subnet name with /27 or larger
B) Subnet named AzureBastionSubnet with /26 or larger
C) No dedicated subnet required
Back: B) Subnet named AzureBastionSubnet with /26 or larger
Extra:
Azure Bastion requires dedicated subnet named AzureBastionSubnet with minimum /26 CIDR. NSG can be associated but not required. Incorrect naming or sizing prevents Bastion deployment.

Reference: Bastion subnet requirements

Front:
An on-premises datacenter requires site-to-site VPN to Azure with 99.9% uptime SLA and automatic failover during gateway maintenance. Which VPN gateway configuration achieves this availability?

A) Single VPN gateway basic SKU
B) Active-active VPN gateway with two tunnels
C) Single tunnel with manual failover
Back: B) Active-active VPN gateway with two tunnels
Extra:
Active-active VPN gateway configuration provides redundancy with automatic failover during maintenance or failures meeting 99.9% SLA. Basic SKU doesn't support active-active. Single tunnel lacks redundancy.

Reference: Highly available VPN

Front:
Your site-to-site VPN requires policy-based IPsec for compatibility with legacy on-premises firewall. Which VPN gateway SKU supports policy-based VPN?

A) Standard or HighPerformance SKUs
B) Basic SKU for policy-based VPN
C) VpnGw1 or higher
Back: B) Basic SKU for policy-based VPN
Extra:
Basic SKU supports policy-based VPN for legacy device compatibility while standard/HighPerformance and VpnGw SKUs support route-based VPN with more features. Policy-based required when legacy devices don't support route-based.

Reference: VPN Gateway SKUs

Front:
A Point-to-Site VPN requires authentication using on-premises Active Directory credentials via RADIUS without Microsoft Entra ID. Which P2S tunnel type and authentication method supports this?

A) SSTP tunnel with Microsoft Entra authentication
B) OpenVPN or IKEv2 tunnel with RADIUS authentication
C) Azure certificate authentication only
Back: B) OpenVPN or IKEv2 tunnel with RADIUS authentication
Extra:
OpenVPN and IKEv2 support RADIUS authentication integrating with on-premises AD via NPS. SSTP supports RADIUS but less flexible than OpenVPN. Certificate authentication doesn't integrate with AD credentials.

Reference: P2S authentication methods

Front:
Remote users require Always On VPN with automatic connection when connected to corporate network. Which Azure P2S VPN feature and client configuration enables this?

A) Manual VPN connection requiring user initiation
B) Always On VPN profile with device tunnel and traffic filters
C) Site-to-site VPN for users
Back: B) Always On VPN profile with device tunnel and traffic filters
Extra:
Always On VPN configures automatic connection with device tunnel for pre-login connectivity and user tunnel for authenticated access, using traffic filters for selective routing. Manual connection doesn't provide automatic connectivity. S2S VPN connects sites, not individual users.

Reference: Always On VPN

Front:
Your organization requires ExpressRoute with 10 Gbps bandwidth and 99.95% SLA. Standard ExpressRoute tier maximum is 10 Gbps but you need higher throughput capacity. Which ExpressRoute option provides 100 Gbps capacity?

A) Multiple standard ExpressRoute circuits
B) ExpressRoute Direct with 100 Gbps port
C) ExpressRoute is limited to 10 Gbps
Back: B) ExpressRoute Direct with 100 Gbps port
Extra:
ExpressRoute Direct provides dedicated 10 Gbps or 100 Gbps physical ports directly connected to Microsoft network, bypassing standard circuit limitations. Multiple standard circuits don't provide single high-bandwidth connection. ExpressRoute supports up to 100 Gbps via Direct.

Reference: ExpressRoute Direct

Front:
An ExpressRoute circuit connects to Azure via private peering. You need to access Microsoft 365 and Dynamics 365 over same circuit without internet breakout. Which peering type enables Microsoft cloud service access?

A) Private peering only can access all Microsoft services
B) Microsoft peering for Microsoft 365 and public cloud services
C) No solution, internet required
Back: B) Microsoft peering for Microsoft 365 and public cloud services
Extra:
Microsoft peering enables access to Microsoft public cloud services (M365, Dynamics 365, Azure public IPs) over ExpressRoute. Private peering only accesses Azure private IP space. Both peerings can coexist on single circuit.

Reference: ExpressRoute peerings

Front:
Your ExpressRoute circuit requires connecting VNets across different Azure regions to on-premises with optimal latency. Which ExpressRoute feature enables cross-region connectivity without multiple circuits?

A) Single ExpressRoute circuit limited to one region
B) ExpressRoute Global Reach connecting remote locations
C) Multiple ExpressRoute circuits required per region
Back: B) ExpressRoute Global Reach connecting remote locations
Extra:
ExpressRoute Global Reach connects circuits in different regions enabling private connectivity between remote locations through Microsoft global network. Single circuit can connect multiple regional VNets. Multiple circuits can also work but Global Reach optimizes multi-region scenarios.

Reference: ExpressRoute Global Reach

Front:
An application requires ultra-low latency to Azure VMs over ExpressRoute. Standard ExpressRoute routing adds slight latency. Which feature bypasses ExpressRoute gateway reducing latency?

A) ExpressRoute standard routing through gateway
B) ExpressRoute FastPath bypassing gateway
C) Latency cannot be reduced further
Back: B) ExpressRoute FastPath bypassing gateway
Extra:
FastPath sends data traffic directly to VMs bypassing ExpressRoute gateway, reducing latency for performance-critical workloads. Standard routing through gateway adds slight latency. FastPath optimizes data path while control plane still uses gateway.

Reference: ExpressRoute FastPath

Front:
ExpressRoute circuit requires encrypting traffic between on-premises and Azure over private peering. Which encryption method secures ExpressRoute private peering traffic?

A) ExpressRoute native encryption not supported
B) IPsec VPN over ExpressRoute private peering (MACsec for link layer)
C) Encryption not needed for private peering
Back: B) IPsec VPN over ExpressRoute private peering (MACsec for link layer)
Extra:
IPsec over ExpressRoute or MACsec for ExpressRoute Direct encrypts traffic over private peering. ExpressRoute itself doesn't encrypt by default. Private peering alone doesn't guarantee encryption meeting security requirements.

Reference: ExpressRoute encryption

Front:
An ExpressRoute connection shows intermittent packet loss. Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) should monitor link health and detect failures faster. How is BFD implemented on ExpressRoute?

A) BFD not supported on ExpressRoute
B) BFD automatically enabled on ExpressRoute circuits
C) Manual BFD configuration required
Back: B) BFD automatically enabled on ExpressRoute circuits
Extra:
BFD is automatically enabled on ExpressRoute private peering providing fast failure detection (sub-second). No manual configuration needed. BFD improves convergence time during network failures.

Reference: ExpressRoute BFD

Front:
Your organization deploys Azure Virtual WAN connecting 50 branch offices and 10 VNets across regions. Which Virtual WAN SKU provides global transit connectivity with inter-hub routing?

A) Basic Virtual WAN limited to single region
B) Standard Virtual WAN with global transit architecture
C) Virtual WAN doesn't support multiple regions
Back: B) Standard Virtual WAN with global transit architecture
Extra:
Standard Virtual WAN enables global transit architecture with any-to-any connectivity across hubs, VNets, and branches. Basic Virtual WAN supports only site-to-site VPN without inter-hub routing. Virtual WAN specifically designed for multi-region scenarios.

Reference: Virtual WAN SKUs

Front:
A Virtual WAN hub requires VPN gateway supporting 1,000 site-to-site connections with 20 Gbps aggregate throughput. Which scale unit configuration meets these requirements?

A) 1 scale unit (500 Mbps)
B) 40 scale units (20 Gbps aggregate)
C) Scale units don't affect throughput
Back: B) 40 scale units (20 Gbps aggregate)
Extra:
Virtual WAN VPN gateway scale units determine aggregate throughput (500 Mbps per unit). 20 Gbps requires 40 scale units. Connection count supported depends on scale units. Scale units directly impact gateway capacity and cost.

Reference: Virtual WAN gateway scale

Front:
Virtual WAN hub requires integrating third-party network virtual appliance for traffic inspection. Which Virtual WAN feature enables NVA integration within hub?

A) NVA cannot integrate with Virtual WAN
B) Virtual WAN NVA integration routing traffic through third-party appliance
C) Separate VNet with NVA only
Back: B) Virtual WAN NVA integration routing traffic through third-party appliance
Extra:
Virtual WAN supports third-party NVA deployment in hub with routing integration enabling traffic inspection for hub-connected resources. NVAs fully integrate with Virtual WAN. Separate VNet defeats centralized Virtual WAN architecture.

Reference: NVA in Virtual WAN

Front:
Your organization connects 4 ExpressRoute circuits in different regions. Each circuit is 10 Gbps. One circuit fails. Which routing configuration ensures traffic automatically uses remaining circuits without manual intervention?

A) Static routes requiring manual failover
B) BGP with AS path prepending or local preference for automatic failover
C) No redundancy configuration possible
Back: B) BGP with AS path prepending or local preference for automatic failover
Extra:
BGP dynamically reroutes traffic through available paths using path attributes (AS path prepending, local preference, MED) providing automatic failover without manual intervention. Static routes require manual updates. ExpressRoute BGP enables resilient multi-circuit deployments.

Reference: ExpressRoute redundancy

Front:
A virtual network gateway for site-to-site VPN shows BGP peer status as disconnected. Diagnosis reveals BGP ASN mismatch between Azure and on-premises. What ASN range is valid for Azure VPN Gateway custom ASN?

A) Only Azure default ASN 65515
B) Valid BGP ASN ranges 64512-65534 (private) or registered public ASN
C) Any 32-bit number
Back: B) Valid BGP ASN ranges 64512-65534 (private) or registered public ASN
Extra:
Azure VPN Gateway supports custom BGP ASN using private ASN ranges (64512-65534) or registered public ASN. Reserved ASNs (65515, 65517) used by Azure. 32-bit ASN requires specific configuration.

Reference: BGP ASN requirements

Front:
Point-to-Site VPN users report intermittent disconnections every 12 hours requiring manual reconnection. Which P2S setting adjustment extends session duration?

A) Default session timeout cannot be adjusted
B) Configure extended idle timeout in VPN gateway P2S configuration
C) Increase VPN gateway SKU
Back: B) Configure extended idle timeout in VPN gateway P2S configuration
Extra:
P2S VPN idle timeout configuration extends session duration before automatic disconnection. Default timeout may be too short for long-running sessions. SKU doesn't affect session timeouts.

Reference: P2S configuration

Front:
An ExpressRoute circuit advertises 200+ routes from on-premises exceeding Azure VNet route limits. Which route optimization prevents route limit issues while maintaining connectivity?

A) Random route removal
B) Route summarization aggregating specific routes into summary routes
C) Disable BGP route advertisement
Back: B) Route summarization aggregating specific routes into summary routes
Extra:
Route summarization reduces advertised route count by aggregating specific prefixes into summary routes fitting within Azure limits without losing connectivity. Random removal creates connectivity issues. Disabling BGP breaks dynamic routing.

Reference: ExpressRoute route optimization

Front:
A multi-tier application requires distributing traffic across 50 backend VMs with session persistence to same VM for 10-minute duration. Which Load Balancer configuration achieves this?

A) Default 5-tuple hash distribution
B) Source IP affinity distribution mode with client IP persistence
C) No session persistence possible
Back: B) Source IP affinity distribution mode with client IP persistence
Extra:
Source IP affinity (session affinity) uses 2-tuple (source IP, destination IP) or 3-tuple (source IP, destination IP, protocol) hash ensuring same client connects to same backend maintaining session stickiness. Default 5-tuple may route sessions to different backends. Load Balancer supports multiple distribution modes.

Reference: Load Balancer distribution modes

Front:
Your organization requires internal Load Balancer for private applications but also needs outbound internet access for backend VMs. Which configuration provides both internal load balancing and outbound connectivity?

A) Internal LB alone provides outbound connectivity
B) Internal LB with separate NAT Gateway or outbound rules
C) Public LB required for any outbound connectivity
Back: B) Internal LB with separate NAT Gateway or outbound rules
Extra:
Internal Load Balancer doesn't provide outbound connectivity by default. NAT Gateway or explicit outbound rules on standard LB enable outbound internet access. Internal LB lacks public IPs for outbound. Multiple LBs (internal and public) or NAT Gateway handle both scenarios.

Reference: Outbound connections

Front:
A Load Balancer requires forwarding specific source IPs to specific backend VMs for admin access (port 3389) independent of load balancing rules. Which LB feature enables this static port forwarding?

A) Load balancing rules don't support specific IPs
B) Inbound NAT rules mapping source IP/port to specific backend
C) Outbound rules configure inbound traffic
Back: B) Inbound NAT rules mapping source IP/port to specific backend
Extra:
Inbound NAT rules map specific frontend IP and port to specific backend VM enabling direct connections like RDP or SSH independent from load balancing distribution. Load balancing rules distribute to all backend pool members. Outbound rules are for outbound, not inbound traffic.

Reference: Inbound NAT rules

Front:
Traffic Manager profiles require routing users to nearest healthy endpoint by latency. One endpoint becomes unhealthy. Which Traffic Manager probe frequency ensures fastest failover detection?

A) 30-second probe interval (default)
B) Fast endpoint monitoring with 10-second interval
C) No health monitoring needed
Back: B) Fast endpoint monitoring with 10-second interval
Extra:
Fast endpoint monitoring (10 seconds) provides quicker health detection and faster failover than standard 30-second interval, critical for latency-sensitive applications. Default 30-second may be too slow. Traffic Manager requires health probes for automatic failover.

Reference: Traffic Manager monitoring

Front:
Virtual machines behind Gateway Load Balancer require traffic inspection by network virtual appliance before reaching actual backend. Which load balancing pattern enables this transparent traffic inspection?

A) Standard Load Balancer without NVA integration
B) Gateway Load Balancer chaining traffic through NVA
C) Application Gateway Layer 7 inspection
Back: B) Gateway Load Balancer chaining traffic through NVA
Extra:
Gateway Load Balancer transparently inserts network virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) in traffic path enabling traffic inspection while maintaining connection to backend pools. Standard LB doesn't provide NVA chaining. App Gateway provides Layer 7 WAF but not arbitrary NVA insertion.

Reference: Gateway Load Balancer

Front:
Application Gateway requires auto-scaling between 2 and 100 compute units based on traffic. Which Application Gateway SKU supports autoscaling?

A) Standard_v1 or WAF_v1 SKU
B) Standard_v2 or WAF_v2 SKU with autoscaling configuration
C) Autoscaling not supported
Back: B) Standard_v2 or WAF_v2 SKU with autoscaling configuration
Extra:
Application Gateway v2 SKU (Standard_v2/WAF_v2) supports autoscaling and zone redundancy. V1 SKU requires manual capacity management. Autoscaling adjusts capacity based on metrics without manual intervention.

Reference: Application Gateway v2

Front:
Application Gateway backend pool includes VMs in multiple subnets with different HTTP settings. One backend requires 60-second timeout while others need 20 seconds. How are different HTTP settings applied per backend?

A) Single HTTP setting applies to all backends
B> Create multiple HTTP settings and routing rules per backend requirement
C) HTTP settings cannot differ per backend
Back: B) Create multiple HTTP settings and routing rules per backend requirement
Extra:
Application Gateway routing rules associate listeners with backend pools using specific HTTP settings, enabling different timeout, cookie, and probe configurations per backend. Single HTTP setting doesn't provide flexibility. Multiple HTTP settings supported per gateway.

Reference: HTTP settings

Front:
Application Gateway requires URL-based routing where /api/* routes to API backend pool and /images/* routes to image backend pool. Which Application Gateway feature implements this path-based routing?

A) Basic routing without path awareness
B) Path-based routing rules with URL path maps
C) Host-based routing only
Back: B) Path-based routing rules with URL path maps
Extra:
Path-based routing rules with URL path maps route requests to different backend pools based on URL path patterns enabling microservices architecture routing. Basic routing lacks path awareness. Host-based routing uses hostnames, not paths.

Reference: URL path-based routing

Front:
Application Gateway health probes show backends unhealthy despite VMs responding to direct HTTP requests. Custom health probe configured but probe URL returns 404. What is the likely cause?

A) Application Gateway malfunction
B) Health probe URL path doesn't exist or returns incorrect status code
C) VMs actually unhealthy
Back: B) Health probe URL path doesn't exist or returns incorrect status code within expected range
Extra:
Health probes require backend responding with 200-399 status code range at specified path. 404 indicates probe path doesn't exist. Direct HTTP requests to different paths may succeed. Probe configuration must match actual backend health endpoint.

Reference: Application Gateway health probes

Front:
Azure Front Door requires end-to-end TLS encryption from client to origin backend. Client connects via HTTPS to Front Door. How do you ensure Front Door to origin also uses HTTPS?

A) Front Door to origin automatically uses HTTPS
B) Configure origin with HTTPS protocol and enforce minimum TLS version
C) TLS termination at Front Door only
Back: B) Configure origin with HTTPS protocol and enforce minimum TLS version
Extra:
End-to-end TLS requires configuring origin with HTTPS protocol ensuring encrypted connection from Front Door to backend. Front Door doesn't automatically enforce origin HTTPS. TLS termination-only decrypts at Front Door sending plaintext to origin.

Reference: Front Door end-to-end TLS

Front:
Azure Front Door caches static content but dynamic API responses are being cached causing stale data. Which Front Door configuration granularly controls caching behavior per route?

A) Global caching cannot be adjusted per route
B) Route rules with caching disabled for dynamic content and enabled for static
C) Disable caching entirely
Back: B) Route rules with caching disabled for dynamic content and enabled for static
Extra:
Front Door route rules enable per-route caching configuration with query string caching behavior, allowing static content caching while bypassing cache for dynamic APIs. Global settings lack granularity. Disabling caching entirely removes performance benefits for static content.

Reference: Front Door caching

Front:
Front Door origin group requires fastest origin selection based on real-time latency measurements. Which routing method achieves this lowest-latency routing?

A) Priority-based routing to primary origin
B) Latency-based routing with latency sensitivity measurements
C) Weighted routing with equal distribution
Back: B) Latency-based routing with latency sensitivity measurements
Extra:
Latency-based routing measures latency to origins in real-time selecting fastest response origin. Priority routing doesn't consider performance. Weighted routing distributes without performance optimization.

Reference: Front Door routing methods

Front:
Azure Front Door URL rewrite required to transform incoming URL /old-path/* to /new-path/* before forwarding to origin. Which Front Door feature implements URL transformation?

A) Routing rules without transformation
B) Rules Engine with URL rewrite actions
C) URL transformation not supported
Back: B) Rules Engine with URL rewrite actions
Extra:
Front Door Rules Engine enables URL rewrite and redirect actions transforming URLs, modifying headers, and implementing custom routing logic. Basic routing doesn't transform URLs. Front Door provides comprehensive traffic manipulation.

Reference: Front Door Rules Engine

Front:
A PaaS service (Azure SQL Database) require private connectivity from specific VNet without public endpoint exposure. Which Azure networking feature provides this private connectivity?

A) Public endpoint with firewall rules
B) Azure Private Endpoint with private IP in VNet
C) Service endpoints with VNet binding
Back: B) Azure Private Endpoint with private IP in VNet
Extra:
Private Endpoint assigns private IP address from VNet to Azure service, eliminating public endpoint exposure and enabling private connectivity. Public endpoints with firewall still expose service to internet. Service endpoints secure traffic but service remains on public IP.

Reference: Private Endpoint

Front:
Multiple workloads in different VNets require private endpoints to Azure Storage. Each VNet has Private DNS zone for storage private endpoint resolution. How should DNS be architected for consistent resolution?

A) Separate DNS zones per VNet creating management complexity
B) Centralized Private DNS zone linked to all VNets with private endpoint
C) No DNS configuration needed
Back: B) Centralized Private DNS zone linked to all VNets with private endpoint
Extra:
Hub Private DNS zone linked to spoke VNets provides centralized DNS management for private endpoint resolution across VNets. Separate zones per VNet multiply management overhead. Private endpoints require DNS to resolve private IPs.

Reference: Private Endpoint DNS

Front:
Your organization exposes self-hosted API as Private Link Service allowing customers to consume via Private Endpoint. Which Standard Load Balancer configuration is required?

A) No load balancer required
B) Internal Standard Load Balancer fronting service backend
C) Public Load Balancer exposed to internet
Back: B) Internal Standard Load Balancer fronting service backend
Extra:
Private Link Service requires Internal Standard Load Balancer fronting workload to expose via Private Link. No load balancer prevents Private Link creation. Public load balancer defeats private access purpose.

Reference: Private Link Service

Front:
On-premises clients require private connectivity to Azure service via Private Link. ExpressRoute connects on-premises to Azure. What additional configuration enables on-premises to resolve private endpoint private IPs?

A) On-premises automatically resolves private endpoints
B) DNS forwarding from on-premises to Azure DNS Private Resolver or DNS servers
C) No DNS configuration needed
Back: B) DNS forwarding from on-premises to Azure DNS Private Resolver or DNS servers
Extra:
On-premises DNS must forward Azure service FQDNs to Azure DNS servers or DNS Private Resolver to resolve private endpoint IPs. Automatic resolution doesn't work without forwarding. Private DNS resolution requires proper DNS configuration.

Reference: On-premises Private Endpoint DNS

Front:
VNet requires restricting Azure Storage access only to specific subnet while blocking other subnets. Which feature provides subnet-level service access control?

A) Network Security Groups (NSGs) blocking storage IPs
B) Service Endpoint with VNet rules on storage account
C) No subnet-level control available
Back: B) Service Endpoint with VNet rules on storage account
Extra:
Service Endpoints enable Azure PaaS services to restrict access to specific VNet subnets using VNet rules. NSGs can't effectively filter Azure PaaS services using service tags. Services support subnet-level access control through service endpoints.

Reference: Service Endpoints

Front:
Azure Storage account requires service endpoint policy enforcing connections only to approved storage accounts, blocking other storage accounts. Which feature provides this service-level filtering?

A) Service endpoints without policy control
B) Service Endpoint Policy with allowed resource list
C) Service policy not supported
Back: B) Service Endpoint Policy with allowed resource list
Extra:
Service Endpoint Policies provide additional control over service endpoints, allowing or denying specific Azure service resources. Basic service endpoints enable VNet-level access without resource-level filtering. Policies add granular control.

Reference: Service Endpoint Policies

Front:
Your architecture requires private endpoint for Azure SQL and service endpoint for Azure Storage in same subnet. Can both features coexist in single subnet?

A) Only one feature per subnet
B) Private endpoints and service endpoints coexist in same subnet
C) Separate VNets required per feature
Back: B) Private endpoints and service endpoints coexist in same subnet
Extra:
Private Endpoints and Service Endpoints are complementary and coexist in same subnet. Different features for different services or different access patterns. Multiple private connectivity options available simultaneously.

Reference: Service endpoint limitations

Front:
A private endpoint connects to Azure SQL Database. On-premises users via VPN report cannot resolve SQL FQDN to private IP. What DNS configuration resolves this?

A) On-premises users automatically resolve private IPs
B) Azure Private DNS zone linked to VNet with conditional forwarding from on-premises
C) No solution for on-premises access
Back: B) Azure Private DNS zone linked to VNet with conditional forwarding from on-premises
Extra:
Private DNS zone linked to VNet contains A record for private endpoint. On-premises DNS forwards queries for Azure service FQDNs to Azure DNS Private Resolver or VNet DNS servers. Automatic resolution doesn't work without forwarding configuration.

Reference: Private DNS integration

Front:
Azure Storage account with service endpoint receives connection attempts from internet despite VNet rules configured. What additional configuration prevents public internet access?

A) Service endpoints alone block public access
B) Disable public network access or configure firewall rules denying internet
C) Public access cannot be prevented
Back: B) Disable public network access or configure firewall rules denying internet
Extra:
Service endpoints secure traffic from VNet but don't disable public endpoint. Disabling public network access or firewall rules blocking internet prevent public access. Service endpoints are one layer of defense requiring additional configuration for complete isolation.

Reference: Storage network security

Front:
Private Link Service exposes internal application to customers via Private Endpoint. Which IP addressing consideration affects Private Link Service design?

A) Private Link Service uses public IPs
B) NAT IP address configuration for Private Link Service
C) No IP addressing considerations
Back: B) NAT IP address configuration for Private Link Service
Extra:
Private Link Service requires NAT IP addresses from subnet for source NAT providing stable IPs for consumer private endpoint connections. Private Link uses private IPs. NAT configuration critical for proper service exposure.

Reference: Private Link NAT IPs

Front:
VNet with multiple subnets requires granular service access where Subnet A can access Azure Storage while Subnet B cannot. How is this subnet-level control implemented?

A) Single service endpoint applies to entire VNet
B) Enable service endpoint on specific subnets and configure VNet rules per subnet
C) No granular subnet control available
Back: B) Enable service endpoint on specific subnets and configure VNet rules per subnet
Extra:
Service endpoints are enabled per subnet with VNet rules on Azure services specifying allowed subnets, providing granular control. Service endpoints don't automatically apply to entire VNet. Subnet-level enablement combined with service VNet rules achieves granular access control.

Reference: Service endpoint configuration

Front:
Private Endpoint for Azure SQL Database requires DNS resolution. Multiple private endpoints exist for different SQL servers. How does DNS resolve correct private IP per SQL server?

A) All private endpoints share same private IP
B) Private DNS zone A records map FQDN to specific private endpoint IP
C) DNS cannot distinguish between private endpoints
Back: B) Private DNS zone A records map FQDN to specific private endpoint IP
Extra:
Each private endpoint receives unique private IP with DNS A record mapping service FQDN to that specific private IP. Multiple private endpoints create multiple DNS records. DNS resolution is specific per service instance.

Reference: Private Endpoint DNS records

Front:
A subnet (10.0.1.0/24) requires Network Security Group allowing HTTP (80) from internet and RDP (3389) from corporate IP 203.0.113.0/24. All other traffic denied. Which NSG rules implement this?

A) No NSG rules needed, default allows all
B) Allow rules for HTTP from Internet service tag and RDP from 203.0.113.0/24, deny all at lower priority
C) Deny rules only
Back: B) Allow rules for HTTP from Internet service tag and RDP from 203.0.113.0/24, deny all at lower priority
Extra:
NSG rules processed by priority with specific allow rules (HTTP from Internet, RDP from source IP) taking precedence before default deny-all. Default NSG rules deny internet inbound. Explicit rules required for allowed traffic.

Reference: NSG rule processing

Front:
Application tier VMs (10.0.2.0/24) require connectivity to database tier (10.0.3.0/24) on port 1433. Both tiers in same VNet. How should security be implemented while minimizing NSG rules?

A) No NSG rules needed for same-VNet traffic
B) Application Security Groups (ASGs) for app and database tiers with NSG rules between ASGs
C) Individual IP-based NSG rules per VM
Back: B) Application Security Groups (ASGs) for app and database tiers with NSG rules between ASGs
Extra:
ASGs group VMs logically enabling NSG rules based on application role instead of IP addresses, simplifying rule management especially with dynamic VMs. Same-VNet traffic isn't automatically secured. IP-based rules per VM don't scale.

Reference: Application Security Groups

Front:
NSG flow logs show high traffic volume but you need to understand traffic patterns, top talkers, and anomalous flows. Which Azure feature analyzes NSG flow logs for insights?

A) Manual flow log analysis
B) Traffic Analytics with AI-powered pattern recognition
C) No flow log analysis available
Back: B) Traffic Analytics with AI-powered pattern recognition
Extra:
Traffic Analytics processes NSG flow logs providing visualizations, anomaly detection, top talkers analysis, and security insights. Manual analysis doesn't scale or detect patterns. Azure provides built-in flow log analytics.

Reference: Traffic Analytics

Front:
Azure Firewall requires centralized management of firewall policies across 20 Azure Firewalls in different regions and subscriptions. Which feature enables centralized policy management?

A) Manual configuration per firewall
B) Azure Firewall Manager with hierarchical policies
C) No centralized management available
Back: B) Azure Firewall Manager with hierarchical policies
Extra:
Azure Firewall Manager provides centralized management with hierarchical policies inheriting base policies while allowing region-specific customization. Manual per-firewall configuration doesn't scale. Firewall Manager specifically designed for multi-firewall governance.

Reference: Azure Firewall Manager

Front:
Azure Firewall requires URL filtering based on FQDN for outbound HTTPS traffic instead of IP-based filtering. Which Azure Firewall rule type enables FQDN-based filtering?

A) Network rules with IP addresses only
B) Application rules with FQDN filtering and TLS inspection
C) FQDN filtering not supported
Back: B) Application rules with FQDN filtering and TLS inspection
Extra:
Application rules support FQDN filtering with TLS inspection for HTTPS traffic, handling dynamic IPs behind domain names. Network rules support IP/port but not FQDNs. FQDN filtering critical for cloud services with changing IPs.

Reference: Azure Firewall FQDN filtering

Front:
Azure Firewall protects spoke VNets in hub-spoke topology. Which Azure Firewall SKU provides intrusion detection and prevention system (IDPS) capabilities?

A) Azure Firewall Basic SKU
B) Azure Firewall Premium with IDPS
C) Standard Firewall with IDPS
Back: B) Azure Firewall Premium with IDPS
Extra:
Azure Firewall Premium provides advanced threat protection including IDPS, TLS inspection, URL filtering, and web categories. Standard firewall lacks advanced security features. Premium SKU required for comprehensive threat protection.

Reference: Azure Firewall Premium

Front:
Virtual WAN hub requires Azure Firewall for secured hub functionality. Which deployment pattern integrates Azure Firewall with Virtual WAN hub?

A) Separate VNet with firewall without hub integration
B) Deploy Azure Firewall inside Virtual WAN hub creating secure hub
C) Firewall cannot integrate with Virtual WAN
Back: B) Deploy Azure Firewall inside Virtual WAN hub creating secure hub
Extra:
Azure Firewall deploys directly in Virtual WAN hub creating secured hub with centralized security policy for all hub-connected resources. Separate VNet defeats centralized Virtual WAN architecture. Firewall integrates with Virtual WAN providing security services.

Reference: Secured Virtual Hub

Front:
Web Application Firewall on Application Gateway shows false positives blocking legitimate traffic. Which WAF configuration allows tuning rules without disabling WAF entirely?

A) Disable WAF for all traffic
B) WAF exclusion lists and custom rules to fine-tune detection
C) WAF rules cannot be adjusted
Back: B) WAF exclusion lists and custom rules to fine-tune detection
Extra:
WAF supports exclusion lists removing specific request attributes from inspection and custom rules overriding managed rule set behavior, reducing false positives while maintaining protection. Disabling WAF removes security. WAF provides extensive tuning capabilities.

Reference: WAF tuning

Front:
Azure Front Door Web Application Firewall requires bot protection and rate limiting per client IP. Which WAF capability provides these advanced protections?

A) Standard WAF managed rules only
B) WAF custom rules with rate limiting and bot manager rule set
C) Bot protection not available
Back: B) WAF custom rules with rate limiting and bot manager rule set
Extra:
WAF custom rules enable rate limiting per client IP and bot manager rule sets provide bot detection and mitigation. Standard managed rules provide OWASP protection. Front Door WAF offers advanced protection capabilities.

Reference: WAF custom rules

Front:
Multiple Application Gateways across regions require consistent WAF policy. Which WAF deployment approach enables policy consistency?

A) Manual policy configuration per gateway
B) WAF policy resource associated with multiple Application Gateways
C) No policy sharing mechanism
Back: B) WAF policy resource associated with multiple Application Gateways
Extra:
WAF policy is standalone resource associable with multiple Application Gateways ensuring consistent protection across deployments. Manual per-gateway configuration creates policy drift. WAF policies provide centralized management.

Reference: WAF policy

Front:
NSG flow logs required for network forensics and security analysis. Where are NSG flow log data stored and how long retained?

A) Flow logs stored only in NSG without external storage
B) Flow logs stored in Azure Storage account with configurable retention
C) No flow log storage or retention
Back: B) Flow logs stored in Azure Storage account with configurable retention
Extra:
NSG flow logs stored in Azure Storage account with configured retention period (1-365 days) for long-term analysis. Logs not stored in NSG itself. Storage and retention configuration required for flow log enablement.

Reference: NSG flow logs

Front:
Azure Firewall processes outbound traffic from 10.0.0.0/16 to internet via SNAT. Destination services block Azure Firewall's public IP. Which firewall configuration provides predictable SNAT IPs for allow-listing?

A) Firewall uses random Azure IPs
B) Explicit outbound SNAT IP configuration with Public IP prefix
C) SNAT IP cannot be controlled
Back: B) Explicit outbound SNAT IP configuration with Public IP prefix
Extra:
Azure Firewall supports explicit SNAT IP configuration using Public IP prefix providing predictable outbound IPs for destination allow-lists. Default uses available firewall IPs. Public IP prefix enables IP range management.

Reference: Azure Firewall SNAT

Front:
WAF on Application Gateway requires CRS 3.2 rule set with specific rules disabled causing false positives. Which WAF configuration supports rule set customization?

A) Managed rule sets cannot be customized
B) Rule set with individual rule disablement and exclusions
C) Only custom rules, no managed rules
Back: B) Rule set with individual rule disablement and exclusions
Extra:
Managed rule sets support per-rule disablement and exclusions allowing fine-tuning while maintaining overall protection. Managed rules customizable. Mix of managed and custom rules provides balanced protection.

Reference: WAF CRS customization

Front:
Azure Firewall threat intelligence alerts show high confidence malicious IPs but firewall configured in alert-only mode. What change enables firewall to block threat intel-identified threats?

A) Alert mode only, no blocking available
B) Enable threat intelligence in Alert and Deny mode
C) Disable threat intelligence
Back: B) Enable threat intelligence in Alert and Deny mode
Extra:
Threat intelligence supports Alert Only (logging) or Alert and Deny (logging + blocking) modes. Alert mode provides visibility without enforcement. Deny mode blocks malicious IPs/FQDNs based on Microsoft threat intelligence.

Reference: Firewall threat intelligence

Front:
Network traffic between Azure regions must remain on Microsoft backbone without traversing public internet. Which networking feature ensures traffic stays on Microsoft network?

A) Public internet routing between regions
B) Azure global network with ExpressRoute Global Reach or VNet peering
C) No private inter-region connectivity
Back: B) Azure global network with ExpressRoute Global Reach or VNet peering
Extra:
Azure global backbone network carries VNet-to-VNet traffic between regions without public internet exposure. ExpressRoute Global Reach extends private connectivity. Inter-region traffic automatically uses Microsoft network.

Reference: Azure global network

Front:
A VNet uses custom DNS servers (10.0.1.4, 10.0.1.5). VMs report cannot resolve Azure-provided names like Azure Storage. What DNS configuration resolves Azure services while using custom DNS?

A) Replace custom DNS with Azure DNS only
B) Custom DNS forward unresolved queries to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16)
C) Azure names cannot be resolved with custom DNS
Back: B) Custom DNS forward unresolved queries to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16)
Extra:
Custom DNS servers forward queries for Azure services to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16) enabling resolution of Azure-provided names while maintaining custom DNS for organization names. Azure-only DNS loses custom resolution. Coexistence requires forwarding configuration.

Reference: Custom DNS forwarding

Front:
ExpressRoute circuit connects to multiple VNets via ExpressRoute gateway. One VNet requires traffic rate limiting to 1 Gbps while others need full circuit bandwidth. How is per-VNet bandwidth controlled?

A) ExpressRoute circuit bandwidth equally splits across VNets
B) Per-VNet bandwidth limits not directly supported; design architecture with traffic management
C) Per-VNet limit configuration available
Back: B) Per-VNet bandwidth limits not directly supported; design architecture with traffic management
Extra:
ExpressRoute doesn't natively support per-VNet bandwidth allocation. Architecture design using separate circuits, traffic management, or VNet-level prioritization required for bandwidth control. Circuit bandwidth shared across connected VNets.

Reference: ExpressRoute gateway constraints

Front:
Virtual network requires multiple address ranges but subnets showing as failed" when ranges overlap. What is the Azure VNet address space limitation causing this?

A) VNets support any overlapping ranges
B) VNet address spaces must not overlap within VNet or with peered VNets
C) Only single address range allowed per VNet"
Back: B) VNet address spaces must not overlap within VNet or with peered VNets
Extra:
VNet address spaces and peered VNet ranges must not overlap to prevent routing conflicts. Multiple non-overlapping ranges supported per VNet. Overlapping ranges cause deployment failures and routing issues.

Reference: VNet address overlap

Front:
Load Balancer backend pool VMs showing intermittent connectivity despite health probes passing. Load balancer logs show SNAT port exhaustion. Which configuration prevents SNAT port exhaustion?

A) Increase VM count without addressing SNAT
B) Add outbound rules with more frontend IPs or implement NAT Gateway
C) SNAT exhaustion cannot be prevented
Back: B) Add outbound rules with more frontend IPs or implement NAT Gateway
Extra:
SNAT port exhaustion resolved by increasing available SNAT ports through additional frontend IPs on outbound rules or offloading to NAT Gateway providing 64K ports per IP. VM count alone doesn't address SNAT. Azure provides scalable outbound connectivity solutions.

Reference: SNAT exhaustion troubleshooting
